title: [InstCombine] Add ctpop(A | B) + ctpop(A & B) -> ctpop(A) + ctpop(B)
summary:
The developers generalized from a specific vector shift pattern to a fundamental bitwise identity: `ctpop(A | B) + ctpop(A & B) == ctpop(A) + ctpop(B)`. The original test case was a special instance where `A` and `B` had no common bits (due to shifts), making `A & B == 0` and `A | B == A + B`. The generalized transform removes this restrictive "no common bits" precondition, recognizing the broader algebraic relationship holds for any `A` and `B`.

The implementation uses commutative pattern matching (`m_c_Or`/`m_c_And`) to handle operand order, and `m_OneUse` guards ensure profitability. This transforms two `ctpop` calls into two simpler ones, reducing computational depth. The new test cases validate the core identity (`ctpop_and_or_combine`), confirm it doesn't over-apply (`ctpop_and_or_combine_neg`), and demonstrate orthogonality with other transforms (`ctpop_rotate_left`).

**Key takeaway**: Identify the underlying mathematical rule behind a narrow optimization, then relax preconditions to apply it generically. Use commutative matching and one-use checks for safe, profitable generalization.
